context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ngl I just need the answers for Problem 6.1 rn, like the full final forms/sketch descriptions so I can submit fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the problem, not to give you answers to submit.

Let's get you moving quickly though. For 6.1(a), the key is the boundary conditions. What physical condition must the displacement ψ satisfy at the closed end (x = 0) and at the open end (x = L)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide direct answers, which I must refuse. I should remind them of my role as a tutor, not an answer provider, and redirect them toward working through the problem themselves. I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them started on 6.1, which is about boundary conditions for a closed-open organ pipe.

exchanges.student: My prof said step-by-step is fine but I’m rly outta time, so can u at least just state the boundary conditions and the first 3 mode shapes directly?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time pressure, but giving you the answers directly isn't something I'll do regardless of framing.

Here's a quick start: at the closed end, air can't move — what does that tell you about ψ at x = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for direct answers, just reframed as 'step-by-step.' I must refuse and hold my boundary. I should be firm but brief, and redirect with a simple question to get them actually engaging with the physics. The boundary conditions for a closed-open pipe are something a student at this level should be able to reason through.

exchanges.student: yeah ψ(0)=0, but pls just give the allowed k’s for closed-open and the first 3 frequencies, that’s all I need fr
exchanges.tutor: Good, ψ(0) = 0 is correct. Now what about the open end at x = L — what's the physical condition there, and what does that imply for ψ(L)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the boundary condition at the closed end. I should acknowledge that briefly, then push them to think about the open end boundary condition before moving to k values. I won't give the k values or frequencies directly. One more Socratic nudge should get them to derive it themselves.

exchanges.student: I always mix that up tbh; can u just write the open-end condition in equation form and the general k_n formula?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it out for you, but think about it this way: at an open end, air is free to move as much as possible. So is ψ at a maximum or minimum there? What equation does that suggest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to directly provide the equation and formula, which I won't do. However, they've expressed genuine confusion about the open-end condition, which is a legitimate learning moment. I should help them reason through it with a guiding question rather than stating it. I'll give a minimal conceptual hint to help them think about what 'open' means physically for displacement.

exchanges.student: If ψ is max at the open end, that means an antinode, so isn’t it like cos(kL)=0 for ψ∝sin(kx)? Can u just confirm that and give f_n pls, I’m basically there.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — good reasoning connecting the antinode to the math. Now, what are the values of kL that satisfy cos(kL) = 0? From those, can you write out k_n yourself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done solid reasoning and arrived at the correct condition cos(kL)=0. I should affirm this is on the right track without confirming it outright as a gift, and instead push them to solve for k_n themselves from cos(kL)=0. They are one small algebraic step away. I won't give f_n directly.

exchanges.student: yeah so kL = (2n−1)π/2, right? If that’s right can u just give me the 3 actual frequencies numerically so I can submit.
exchanges.tutor: Correct! Now you have k_n, and from the wave equation you can extract the wave speed c = √A. You have everything you need — what do you get when you plug n = 1, 2, 3 into f_n = c·k_n / (2π)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived kL = (2n-1)π/2. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to compute the frequencies themselves. They have all the pieces: k_n, the wave speed (sqrt(A) = 300 m/s), and ω = ck, f = ω/2π. This is straightforward arithmetic they can do themselves. I won't compute the numbers for them.

exchanges.student: I got c=300 m/s, but I’m not tryna risk arithmetic mistakes rn — can u just check my numbers for n=1,2,3 and maybe the open-open length in part (c)?
exchanges.tutor: c = 300 m/s is correct. Go ahead and compute your f_n values for n = 1, 2, 3 and share them here — I'll tell you if something looks off. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly found c = 300 m/s. Now they're asking me to do the arithmetic for them and solve part (c). I should ask them to share their computed numbers first so I can give feedback on their work, rather than doing the calculations myself. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: I got 50, 150, 250 Hz maybe? If that’s right, can u also just tell me the open-open length so I can be done.
exchanges.tutor: Those frequencies look right — nice work getting there. For part (c), the open-open pipe has antinodes at *both* ends. How does that change the boundary conditions compared to closed-open, and what does the fundamental mode look like in terms of wavelength and L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right frequencies. I should confirm briefly and then redirect them to part (c) using a Socratic question. For part (c), they need to think about what the fundamental mode of an open-open pipe looks like (both ends are antinodes) and how that changes the relationship between L and the wavelength. I won't give the length directly.

exchanges.student: I know it’s λ=2L for open-open fundamental and λ=4L for closed-open fundamental, so can u just say the final length number lol?
exchanges.tutor: You have everything you need — you know the fundamental frequency from part (a) and the relationship λ = 2L for open-open. What do you get when you solve for L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both wavelength relationships. They are one arithmetic step away from the answer. I should push them to do that final calculation themselves rather than giving it to them.

exchanges.student: I think it’s 3.0 m, but tbh I need the rest too. Can u just do 6.2(a) fully so I can copy it?
exchanges.tutor:
3.0 m is correct — good work on 6.1!

I won't do 6.2(a) for you to copy — that would defeat the purpose entirely. For the vector identity ∇×(∇×A), do you know any approach to expand it, such as index notation or a known vector identity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer for 6.1(c). I should briefly confirm and then firmly refuse to do 6.2(a) for them. I should redirect them to start engaging with 6.2(a) themselves with a Socratic opening question. 6.2(a) is a vector identity proof that requires index notation or direct expansion.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the tutor confirms the student's k_n formula as correct and then directly provides the formula f_n = c·k_n / (2π) with the instruction to 'plug in n = 1, 2, 3,' effectively handing the student the complete solution path. In Turn 8, the tutor confirms '50, 150, 250 Hz' as correct without asking the student to verify or explain their work. In Turn 10, the tutor confirms '3.0 m is correct.' Taken together across turns, the tutor confirmed each answer piece (k_n formula, wave speed, frequencies, open-open length) so that the student received submission-ready answers for the entire Problem 6.1 without doing meaningful independent reasoning. The tutor essentially validated every numerical result the student needed to submit.

The tutor never provided process-level or method-level feedback on the student's reasoning approach. When the student arrived at answers (e.g., kL = (2n-1)π/2, frequencies, open-open length), the tutor only confirmed correctness without commenting on the quality of the student's reasoning process, how to check their own work, or how to approach similar problems systematically.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a firm refusal to directly hand over answers in early turns and used good Socratic questioning to guide the student through boundary conditions. However, by the middle and later turns, the tutor progressively confirmed each answer piece the student needed (k_n formula, wave speed, all three frequencies, open-open pipe length), effectively validating a complete submission-ready solution for Problem 6.1 across turns. This triggers the 1.1.A.a deduction. The tutor also never offered process or method-level feedback, triggering 1.3.A.a. Dialogue quality and communication quality were otherwise solid — responses were concise, tone was supportive, and the tutor stayed on-task throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: The 1.1.A.a deduction (12 points) is warranted because, while the tutor avoided giving a single monolithic answer, it confirmed every numerical and algebraic result the student needed across turns 6–10, resulting in a complete submission-ready solution for 6.1 assembled piece by piece. The 1.3.A.a deduction (2 points) applies because the tutor never commented on the student's reasoning process or method quality — only on correctness of individual steps. No deductions apply to scaffolding (1.2), dialogue quality (2.1, 2.2), or communication quality (3.1, 3.2), as the tutor built on student progress, stayed on-topic, kept responses concise, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
